citations.title: Effect of breathwork on stress and mental health: A meta-analysis of randomised-controlled trials
citations.abstract: Abstract Deliberate control of the breath (breathwork) has recently received an unprecedented surge in public interest and breathing techniques have therapeutic potential to improve mental health. Our meta-analysis primarily aimed to evaluate the efficacy of breathwork through examining whether, and to what extent, breathwork interventions were associated with lower levels of self-reported/subjective stress compared to non-breathwork controls. We searched PsycInfo, PubMed, ProQuest, Scopus, Web

citations.support_rationale: This reference discusses the theoretical mechanism of 'ghost ICs' caused by rank deficiency and incorrect re-referencing, but it does not provide the specific empirical statistics (0.4 abnormal channels, 1.6 extracted ICs) claimed in the text.

citations.abstract: The need to breathe links the mammalian olfactory system inextricably to the respiratory rhythms that draw air through the nose. citations.title: Effect of breathwork on stress and mental health: A meta-analysis of randomised-controlled trials
citations.abstract: Abstract Deliberate control of the breath (breathwork) has recently received an unprecedented surge in public interest and breathing techniques have therapeutic potential to improve mental health. Our meta-analysis primarily aimed to evaluate the efficacy of breathwork through examining whether, and to what extent, breathwork interventions were associated with lower levels of self-reported/subjective stress compared to non-breathwork controls. We searched PsycInfo, PubMed, ProQuest, Scopus, Web
citations.support_rationale: This reference is a meta-analysis on the psychological effects of breathwork on stress and anxiety, containing no data on EEG spectral power, spatiotemporal clusters, or comparisons between WHMB and Baseline conditions.

citations.title: Voluntary activation of the sympathetic nervous system and attenuation of the innate immune response in humans
citations.abstract: Excessive or persistent proinflammatory cytokine production plays a central role in autoimmune diseases. Acute activation of the sympathetic nervous system attenuates the innate immune response. However, both the autonomic nervous system and innate immune system are regarded as systems that cannot be voluntarily influenced. Herein, we evaluated the effects of a training program on the autonomic nervous system and innate immune response. Healthy volunteers were randomized to either the interventi
citations.support_rationale: The reference investigates the effects of meditation and breathing techniques on the sympathetic nervous system and immune response in humans, whereas the claim concerns specific spatiotemporal clusters of source spectral power in a 'Naives' group under WHMB versus Baseline conditions. There is no methodological or data overlap regarding EEG/MEG spectral analysis or the specific frequency bands mentioned in the claim.

citations.title: Relationship between Hyperventilation-Induced Electroencephalographic Changes and PCO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;/sub&gt; Level
citations.abstract: BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE: We conducted this study to define the relationship between the hyperventilation-induced EEG changes (HV-EEG changes) and PCO2. METHODS: In consecutive EEG recordings of 190 patients, we gathered data on PCO2 during the hyperventilation procedure. The data included baseline PCO2 (B-PCO2), PCO2 after 5 min of hyperventilation (5 min-PCO2), the mean value of the PCO2 (M-PCO2), and the difference between B-PCO2 and 5 min-PCO2 (ΔPCO2). We divided the enrolled patients into two
citations.support_rationale: The reference investigates EEG changes induced by hyperventilation and their relationship to PCO2 levels, whereas the claim concerns high gamma-band connectivity during cold immersion. The study population, experimental stimulus, and specific frequency bands analyzed do not align with the claim.

citations.title: Understanding the Role of Sensorimotor Beta Oscillations
citations.abstract: Beta oscillations have been predominantly observed in sensorimotor cortices and basal ganglia structures and they are thought to be involved in somatosensory processing and motor control. Although beta activity is a distinct feature of healthy and pathological sensorimotor processing, the role of this rhythm is still under debate. Here we review recent findings about the role of beta oscillations during experimental manipulations (i.e., drugs and brain stimulation) and their alteration in aging
citations.support_rationale: The reference exclusively discusses beta oscillations (typically 13-30 Hz) in sensorimotor contexts, whereas the claim specifically concerns alpha frequency band (8-13 Hz) differences between WHM and Baseline groups.

Here we review recent findings about the role of beta oscillations during experimental manipulations (i.e., drugs and brain stimulation) and their alteration in aging

citations.title: Respiration modulates oscillatory neural network activity at rest
citations.abstract: Despite recent advances in understanding how respiration affects neural signalling to influence perception, cognition, and behaviour, it is yet unclear to what extent breathing modulates brain oscillations at rest. We acquired respiration and resting state magnetoencephalography (MEG) data from human participants to investigate if, where, and how respiration cyclically modulates oscillatory amplitudes (2 to 150 Hz). Using measures of phase-amplitude coupling, we show respiration-modulated brain
citations.support_rationale: This study investigates respiration-brain coupling using MEG and does not measure or report changes in end-tidal CO2 (EtCO₂) levels.

citations.title: Effect of breathwork on stress and mental health: A meta-analysis of randomised-controlled trials
citations.abstract: Abstract Deliberate control of the breath (breathwork) has recently received an unprecedented surge in public interest and breathing techniques have therapeutic potential to improve mental health. citations.title: Beta-band oscillations — signalling the status quo?
Beyond the Status Quo: A Role for Beta Oscillations in Endogenous Content (Re)Activation
citations.abstract: Among the rhythms of the brain, oscillations in the beta frequency range (∼13-30 Hz) have been considered the most enigmatic. Traditionally associated with sensorimotor functions, beta oscillations have recently become more broadly implicated in top-down processing, long-range communication, and preservation of the current brain state. Here, we extend and refine these views based on accumulating new findings of content-specific beta-synchronization during endogenous information processing in wor
citations.support_rationale: The reference supports the general role of beta rhythms in top-down processing and endogenous activation, but it focuses on working memory and decision-making rather than specifically addressing motor/respiratory contexts or the predictive timing loops required for paced breathing.

empirical.evidence_summary: The cited review provides a theoretical basis for beta oscillations in endogenous control but lacks specific evidence regarding their role in motor/respiratory synchronization or the predictive timing mechanisms essential for guided breathing.

empirical.claim: After breathing movements are completed, beta power rebounds (synchronisation), potentially signalling a return to the resting state or stabilisation of the sensorimotor system.

empirical.rationale: The text explicitly states that after breathing movements are completed, beta power rebounds (synchronisation), potentially signalling a return to the resting state or stabilisation of the sensorimotor system.

citations.title: The superior parietal lobule of primates: a sensory-motor hub for interaction with the environment
citations.abstract: The superior parietal lobule of the macaque monkey occupies the postero-medial part of the parietal lobe and plays a crucial role in the integration of different sources of information (from visual, motor and somatosensory brain regions) for the purpose of high-level cognitive functions, as perception for action. This region encompasses the intraparietal sulcus and the parieto-occipital sulcus and includes also the precuneate cortex in the mesial surface of the hemisphere. It hosts several areas
citations.support_rationale: This review focuses on macaque anatomy and connectivity rather than human large-sample resting-state mapping, and it does not address the specific differentiation of SPL subregions into somatosensory, visual, and frontoparietal control networks.

citations.title: Investigating the sub-regions of the superior parietal cortex using functional magnetic resonance imaging connectivity
citations.abstract: OBJECTIVES: Traditionally, the superior parietal lobule (SPL) is usually investigated as one region of interest, particularly in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies. However, cytoarchitectonic analysis has shown that the SPL has a complex, heterogeneous topology that comprises more than seven sub-regions. Since previous studies have shown how the SPL is significantly involved in different neurological functions-such as visuomotor, cognitive, sensory, higher order, working memory
citations.support_rationale: This study explicitly uses large-sample resting-state fMRI (N=198) to map multiple SPL subregions and demonstrates their differentiated links to visual, attention, sensorimotor, and salience networks, directly validating the claim's methodology and findings.

citations.title: A DCM study of spectral asymmetries in feedforward and feedback connections between visual areas V1 and V4 in the monkey
citations.abstract: This paper reports a dynamic causal modeling study of electrocorticographic (ECoG) data that addresses functional asymmetries between forward and backward connections in the visual cortical hierarchy. Specifically, we ask whether forward connections employ gamma-band frequencies, while backward connections preferentially use lower (beta-band) frequencies. We addressed this question by modeling empirical cross spectra using a neural mass model equipped with superficial and deep pyramidal cell pop
citations.support_rationale: This paper investigates spectral asymmetries in visual area connections using ECoG and dynamic causal modeling in monkeys, which is methodologically and thematically unrelated to large-sample human resting-state mapping of the SPL.

citations.title: A DCM study of spectral asymmetries in feedforward and feedback connections between visual areas V1 and V4 in the monkey
citations.abstract: This paper reports a dynamic causal modeling study of electrocorticographic (ECoG) data that addresses functional asymmetries between forward and backward connections in the visual cortical hierarchy. We addressed this question by modeling empirical cross spectra using a neural mass model equipped with superficial and deep pyramidal cell pop

citations.abstract: Working memory (WM) activity is not as stationary or sustained as previously thought. There are brief bursts of gamma (~50-120 Hz) and beta (~20-35 Hz) oscillations, the former linked to stimulus information in spiking. We examined these dynamics in relation to readout and control mechanisms of WM. Monkeys held sequences of two objects in WM to match to subsequent sequences. Changes in beta and gamma bursting suggested their distinct roles. In anticipation of having to use an object for the matc
citations.support_rationale: This study focuses on gamma and beta bursts in monkeys during working memory tasks, lacking any mention of human novices, anterior gamma (AF8), or group contrasts between skill levels.

citations.abstract: Among the rhythms of the brain, oscillations in the beta frequency range (∼13-30 Hz) have been considered the most enigmatic. However, cytoarchitectonic analysis has shown that the SPL has a complex, heterogeneous topology that comprises more than seven sub-regions. Since previous studies have shown how the SPL is significantly involved in different neurological functions-such as visuomotor, cognitive, sensory, higher order, working memory
citations.support_rationale: The study focuses on fMRI connectivity of the superior parietal cortex sub-regions and does not address high-frequency (80-200 Hz) oscillations, task-locked sensory activation, or Beta feedback/feedforward processing mechanisms.

citations.title: Structural connectivity of the precuneus and its relation to resting-state networks
citations.abstract: The precuneus is an association area in the posteromedial cortex (PMC) that is involved in high-order cognitive functions through integrating multi-modal information. Previous studies have shown that the precuneus is functionally heterogeneous and subdivided into several subfields organized by the anterior-posterior and ventral-dorsal axes. Further, the precuneus forms the structural core of brain connectivity as a rich-club hub and overlaps with the default mode network (DMN) as the functional

Here, we propose that, under arousal, local glutamate levels signal the current strength of a representation and interact with norepinephrine (NE) to enhance high priority representations and out-compete or suppress lower priority representations. citations.title: Beta-band oscillations — signalling the status quo?
Beyond the Status Quo: A Role for Beta Oscillations in Endogenous Content (Re)Activation
citations.abstract: Among the rhythms of the brain, oscillations in the beta frequency range (∼13-30 Hz) have been considered the most enigmatic. Traditionally associated with sensorimotor functions, beta oscillations have recently become more broadly implicated in top-down processing, long-range communication, and preservation of the current brain state. Here, we extend and refine these views based on accumulating new findings of content-specific beta-synchronization during endogenous information processing in wor
citations.support_rationale: The reference explicitly supports the 'status quo' hypothesis by describing beta oscillations as mechanisms for preserving the current brain state and maintaining sensorimotor or cognitive sets. However, it argues for a more active role ('endogenous content (re)activation') rather than merely passive maintenance, suggesting the claim slightly underestimates the dynamic, content-specific nature of beta activity described in the paper.

contextual.evidence_summary: The cited review directly validates the core assertion that beta rhythms maintain the current cognitive or sensorimotor state, while adding nuance regarding the active reactivation of endogenous content rather than simple preservation.

citations.title: The gateway hypothesis of rostral prefrontal cortex (area 10) function
Gamma and beta bursts during working memory readout suggest roles in its volitional control
citations.abstract: Working memory (WM) activity is not as stationary or sustained as previously thought. There are brief bursts of gamma (~50-120 Hz) and beta (~20-35 Hz) oscillations, the former linked to stimulus information in spiking. We examined these dynamics in relation to readout and control mechanisms of WM. Monkeys held sequences of two objects in WM to match to subsequent sequences. Changes in beta and gamma bursting suggested their distinct roles. In anticipation of having to use an object for the matc
citations.support_rationale: The reference discusses gamma and beta bursts related to working memory readout and control, which aligns with the 'tracking alternative actions' aspect of the claim. However, it does not explicitly address the encoding of 'relative uncertainty' in this context.

citations.title: The role of rostral prefrontal cortex in prospective memory: A voxel-based lesion study
citations.abstract: Patients with lesions in rostral prefrontal cortex (PFC) often experience problems in everyday-life situations requiring multitasking. A key cognitive component that is critical in multitasking situations is prospective memory, defined as the ability to carry out an intended action after a delay period filled with unrelated activity. The few functional imaging studies investigating prospective memory have shown consistent activation in both medial and lateral rostral PFC but also in more posteri
citations.support_rationale: This study focuses on prospective memory and time-based tasks in the rostral prefrontal cortex, without mentioning the encoding of relative uncertainty or the tracking of alternative actions.

citations.title: The neurocomputational bases of explore-exploit decision-making
Medial and Lateral Networks in Anterior Prefrontal Cortex Support Metacognitive Ability for Memory and Perception
citations.abstract: Convergent evidence indicates that frontopolar Brodmann area 10, and more generally the anterior prefrontal cortex (aPFC), supports the human capacity to monitor and reflect on cognition and experience. An important unanswered question, however, is whether aPFC is a homogeneous region that supports a general-purpose metacognitive ability or whether there could be regional specialization within aPFC with respect to specific types of metacognitive processes. Previous studies suggest that the later
citations.support_rationale: The reference supports the idea that anterior prefrontal cortex regions are involved in metacognitive monitoring of memory and perception, which relates to the 'tracking' aspect. It does not, however, explicitly discuss the encoding of 'relative uncertainty'.

citations.abstract: Little is known about how the modulation of the interoceptive focus impacts the neural correlates of high-level social processes, such as synchronization mechanisms. Therefore, the current study aims to explore the intraindividual electrophysiological (EEG) patterns induced by the interoceptive focus on breath when performing cognitive and motor tasks requiring interpersonal synchronization. A sample of 28 healthy caucasian adults was recruited and asked to perform two tasks requiring interperso
citations.support_rationale: This reference supports the link between delta activity and interoceptive focus (breath attention) in frontal/temporo-central regions, which aligns with the claim's focus on interoception. However, it does not address the specific distinction regarding hyperventilation-induced changes or the 'slow control' mechanism in L2, limiting its direct support for the full claim.

citations.title: Hydroelectric structures studies using 3-dimensional methods
citations.abstract: Cognitive control allows behavior to be guided according to environmental contexts and internal goals. During cognitive control tasks, fMRI analyses typically reveal increased activation in frontal and parietal networks, and EEG analyses reveal increased amplitude of neural oscillations in the delta/theta band (2-3, 4-7 Hz) in frontal electrodes. Previous studies proposed that theta-band activity reflects the maintenance of rules associating stimuli to appropriate actions (i.e., the rule set), w
citations.support_rationale: This reference supports the interpretation of frontopolar delta as a 'slow control' signal related to rule abstraction and context maintenance, which matches the claim's functional description. It fails to support the specific exclusion of hyperventilation as a confounding factor, as it does not discuss respiratory artifacts or hyperventilation.

contextual.evidence_summary: The cited references collectively support the functional interpretation of frontopolar delta as a control signal for internal states (interoception and rule abstraction), but neither provides evidence to rule out hyperventilation as an alternative explanation for the observed EEG changes.

citations.support_rationale: The abstract explicitly states that the precuneus is involved in high-order cognitive functions through 'integrating multi-modal information,' directly supporting the first part of the claim. citations.title: Coupling of respiration and attention via the locus coeruleus: Effects of meditation and pranayama
citations.abstract: Abstract The locus coeruleus (LC) has established functions in both attention and respiration. Good attentional performance requires optimal levels of tonic LC activity, and must be matched to task consistently. LC neurons are chemosensitive, causing respiratory phrenic nerve firing to increase frequency with higher CO 2 levels, and as CO 2 level varies with the phase of respiration, tonic LC activity should exhibit fluctuations at respiratory frequency. Top‐down modulation of tonic LC activity

citations.title: Does the Wim Hof Method have a beneficial impact on physiological and psychological outcomes in healthy and non-healthy participants? A systematic review
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Wim Hof, also known as the iceman, developed a method called Wim Hof Method (WHM) which he claims to have several benefits on physical and mental health. The aim of this systematic review is to identify and synthesise the results of the studies conducted on WHM on physiological and psychological health-related outcomes. MATERIALS AND METHODS: This systematic review followed the PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews. The protocol was registered in the International Prospective Re
citations.support_rationale: This systematic review explicitly concludes that the Wim Hof Method (which includes cold exposure and breathing) may reduce inflammation by increasing IL-10 and decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokines, directly supporting the claim's core assertion.

citations.title: Involvement of Lactate and Pyruvate in the Anti-Inflammatory Effects Exerted by Voluntary Activation of the Sympathetic Nervous System
citations.abstract: We recently demonstrated that the sympathetic nervous system can be voluntarily activated following a training program consisting of cold exposure, breathing exercises, and meditation. This resulted in profound attenuation of the systemic inflammatory response elicited by lipopolysaccharide (LPS) administration. Herein, we assessed whether this training program affects the plasma metabolome and if these changes are linked to the immunomodulatory effects observed. A total of 224 metabolites were
citations.support_rationale: The study demonstrates that a training program involving cold exposure and breathing exercises leads to a profound attenuation of the systemic inflammatory response via the Cori cycle and increased IL-10, providing direct mechanistic evidence for the claim.

aspirational.evidence_summary: Both cited references provide direct evidence supporting the claim, with one offering a systematic review of multiple trials showing reduced inflammation and the other detailing the specific metabolic mechanisms (lactate/pyruvate/IL-10) by which cold exposure and breathing exercises achieve anti-inflammatory effects.

citations.title: Nasal Respiration Entrains Human Limbic Oscillations and Modulates Cognitive Function
citations.abstract: The need to breathe links the mammalian olfactory system inextricably to the respiratory rhythms that draw air through the nose. In rodents and other small animals, slow oscillations of local field potential activity are driven at the rate of breathing (∼2-12 Hz) in olfactory bulb and cortex, and faster oscillatory bursts are coupled to specific phases of the respiratory cycle. These dynamic rhythms are thought to regulate cortical excitability and coordinate network interactions, helping to sha
citations.support_rationale: The abstract focuses on respiratory entrainment of limbic oscillations and cognitive function in humans with epilepsy, offering no evidence regarding the feasibility or difficulty of sample size calculations for the current study.

citations.support_rationale: This review synthesizes findings on respiratory-modulated brain oscillations and their link to psychiatric disorders, without mentioning statistical power, incidence rates, or sample size constraints.

citations.title: Auditory cortical delta-entrainment interacts with oscillatory power in multiple fronto-parietal networks
citations.abstract: The timing of slow auditory cortical activity aligns to the rhythmic fluctuations in speech. This entrainment is considered to be a marker of the prosodic and syllabic encoding of speech, and has been shown to correlate with intelligibility. Yet, whether and how auditory cortical entrainment is influenced by the activity in other speech-relevant areas remains unknown. Using source-localized MEG data, we quantified the dependency of auditory entrainment on the state of oscillatory activity in fro
citations.support_rationale: The reference establishes that auditory delta entrainment interacts with oscillatory activity in fronto-parietal networks, specifically noting modulation by beta power in orbitofrontal areas. While this confirms the existence of cross-frequency interactions in frontal regions that could theoretically influence frontopolar signals, it does not explicitly state that speech prosody-locked delta entrainment inflates the frontopolar slow-band signal or acts as a specific confound for L2 delta effects.

noise.evidence_summary: The cited evidence supports the general mechanism of cross-frequency interactions between auditory delta and frontal oscillations, providing a plausible basis for the confound hypothesis. However, it lacks specific confirmation that this interaction results in the inflation of the frontopolar slow-band signal in the context of L2 processing, leaving the claim partially supported by theoretical plausibility rather than direct empirical validation.

citations.title: Effect of breathwork on stress and mental health: A meta-analysis of randomised-controlled trials
citations.abstract: Abstract Deliberate control of the breath (breathwork) has recently received an unprecedented surge in public interest and breathing techniques have therapeutic potential to improve mental health. Our meta-analysis primarily aimed to evaluate the efficacy of breathwork through examining whether, and to what extent, breathwork interventions were associated with lower levels of self-reported/subjective stress compared to non-breathwork controls. We searched PsycInfo, PubMed, ProQuest, Scopus, Web
citations.support_rationale: The reference discusses the efficacy of breathwork on stress and mental health in humans, whereas the claim concerns the measurement of neural endpoints in relation to the Whole Brain Model (WHM), likely in a different context or species.

citations.title: Does the Wim Hof Method have a beneficial impact on physiological and psychological outcomes in healthy and non-healthy participants? A systematic review
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Wim Hof, also known as the iceman, developed a method called Wim Hof Method (WHM) which he claims to have several benefits on physical and mental health. The aim of this systematic review is to identify and synthesise the results of the studies conducted on WHM on physiological and psychological health-related outcomes. citations.abstract: BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE: We conducted this study to define the relationship between the hyperventilation-induced EEG changes (HV-EEG changes) and PCO2. METHODS: In consecutive EEG recordings of 190 patients, we gathered data on PCO2 during the hyperventilation procedure. The data included baseline PCO2 (B-PCO2), PCO2 after 5 min of hyperventilation (5 min-PCO2), the mean value of the PCO2 (M-PCO2), and the difference between B-PCO2 and 5 min-PCO2 (ΔPCO2). We divided the enrolled patients into two

citations.title: Nasal Respiration Entrains Human Limbic Oscillations and Modulates Cognitive Function
citations.abstract: The need to breathe links the mammalian olfactory system inextricably to the respiratory rhythms that draw air through the nose. In rodents and other small animals, slow oscillations of local field potential activity are driven at the rate of breathing (∼2-12 Hz) in olfactory bulb and cortex, and faster oscillatory bursts are coupled to specific phases of the respiratory cycle. These dynamic rhythms are thought to regulate cortical excitability and coordinate network interactions, helping to sha
citations.support_rationale: The abstract confirms that oscillatory power diminishes when breathing is diverted from the nose to the mouth, supporting the mechanism that nasal breathing alters brain activity; however, it does not address the specific clinical outcome of reducing mouth-opening episodes or EEG motion artifacts in the context of WHM instructions.

empirical.evidence_summary: While the cited references establish that nasal breathing modulates brain oscillations differently than mouth breathing, neither study provides evidence regarding the reduction of mouth-opening episodes or the mitigation of specific motion and EMG artifacts during Phase 2 of WHM instructions.

citations.title: Does better regulation depoliticise policymaking? A case study of standardised packaging of tobacco

citations.abstract: Historically, the locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) system has been implicated in arousal, but recent findings suggest that this system plays a more complex and specific role in the control of behavior than investigators previously thought. We review neurophysiological and modeling studies in monkey that support a new theory of LC-NE function. LC neurons exhibit two modes of activity, phasic and tonic. Phasic LC activation is driven by the outcome of task-related decision processes and is p

citations.title: Beta-band oscillations — signalling the status quo?
Beyond the Status Quo: A Role for Beta Oscillations in Endogenous Content (Re)Activation
citations.abstract: Among the rhythms of the brain, oscillations in the beta frequency range (∼13-30 Hz) have been considered the most enigmatic. Traditionally associated with sensorimotor functions, beta oscillations have recently become more broadly implicated in top-down processing, long-range communication, and preservation of the current brain state. Here, we extend and refine these views based on accumulating new findings of content-specific beta-synchronization during endogenous information processing in wor
citations.support_rationale: The reference supports the general role of beta rhythms in top-down processing and endogenous activation, but it focuses on working memory and decision-making rather than specifically addressing motor/respiratory contexts or the predictive timing loops required for paced breathing.

empirical.evidence_summary: The cited review provides a theoretical basis for beta oscillations in endogenous control but lacks specific evidence regarding their role in motor/respiratory synchronization or the predictive timing mechanisms essential for guided breathing.

citations.title: The superior parietal lobule of primates: a sensory-motor hub for interaction with the environment
citations.abstract: The superior parietal lobule of the macaque monkey occupies the postero-medial part of the parietal lobe and plays a crucial role in the integration of different sources of information (from visual, motor and somatosensory brain regions) for the purpose of high-level cognitive functions, as perception for action. This region encompasses the intraparietal sulcus and the parieto-occipital sulcus and includes also the precuneate cortex in the mesial surface of the hemisphere. It hosts several areas
citations.support_rationale: This review focuses on the anatomical and functional organization of the superior parietal lobule for sensorimotor integration but does not discuss gamma oscillations, feedforward/feedback dynamics, or precision-weighting mechanisms.

citations.title: Investigating the sub-regions of the superior parietal cortex using functional magnetic resonance imaging connectivity
citations.abstract: OBJECTIVES: Traditionally, the superior parietal lobule (SPL) is usually investigated as one region of interest, particularly in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies. However, cytoarchitectonic analysis has shown that the SPL has a complex, heterogeneous topology that comprises more than seven sub-regions. Since previous studies have shown how the SPL is significantly involved in different neurological functions-such as visuomotor, cognitive, sensory, higher order, working memory
citations.support_rationale: While this study confirms the involvement of the superior parietal cortex in attention and salience networks, it relies on resting-state fMRI connectivity and lacks any analysis of gamma-band activity or feedforward processing mechanisms.

citations.title: A DCM study of spectral asymmetries in feedforward and feedback connections between visual areas V1 and V4 in the monkey
citations.abstract: This paper reports a dynamic causal modeling study of electrocorticographic (ECoG) data that addresses functional asymmetries between forward and backward connections in the visual cortical hierarchy. Specifically, we ask whether forward connections employ gamma-band frequencies, while backward connections preferentially use lower (beta-band) frequencies. We addressed this question by modeling empirical cross spectra using a neural mass model equipped with superficial and deep pyramidal cell pop
citations.support_rationale: This study directly demonstrates that forward (feedforward) connections in the visual hierarchy predominantly employ gamma-band frequencies, which aligns with the claim's assertion about gamma being associated with feedforward activity.

citations.title: Coupling of respiration and attention via the locus coeruleus: Effects of meditation and pranayama
citations.abstract: Abstract The locus coeruleus (LC) has established functions in both attention and respiration. LC neurons are chemosensitive, causing respiratory phrenic nerve firing to increase frequency with higher CO 2 levels, and as CO 2 level varies with the phase of respiration, tonic LC activity should exhibit fluctuations at respiratory frequency. Top‐down modulation of tonic LC activity

citations.title: Does the Wim Hof Method have a beneficial impact on physiological and psychological outcomes in healthy and non-healthy participants? A systematic review
citations.abstract: INTRODUCTION: Wim Hof, also known as the iceman, developed a method called Wim Hof Method (WHM) which he claims to have several benefits on physical and mental health. The aim of this systematic review is to identify and synthesise the results of the studies conducted on WHM on physiological and psychological health-related outcomes. MATERIALS AND METHODS: This systematic review followed the PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews. The protocol was registered in the International Prospective Re
citations.support_rationale: The review confirms that the Wim Hof Method (combining cold exposure and breathing) reduces inflammation in healthy and non-healthy participants, which aligns with the claim's core mechanism. However, it is a systematic review of existing studies rather than primary experimental human endotoxemia data, and it does not explicitly isolate 'training' effects or quantify the magnitude as 'significant' in the specific context of endotoxemia.

empirical.evidence_summary: The cited review provides supportive evidence that cold exposure and breathing exercises can reduce inflammation in humans, validating the general mechanism. However, the evidence is indirect as it relies on a synthesis of various studies rather than direct experimental data from human endotoxemia models, and it does not explicitly confirm the 'significant' enhancement in that specific experimental context.

Our meta-analysis primarily aimed to evaluate the efficacy of breathwork through examining whether, and to what extent, breathwork interventions were associated with lower levels of self-reported/subjective stress compared to non-breathwork controls. citations.abstract: Historically, the locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) system has been implicated in arousal, but recent findings suggest that this system plays a more complex and specific role in the control of behavior than investigators previously thought. We review neurophysiological and modeling studies in monkey that support a new theory of LC-NE function. LC neurons exhibit two modes of activity, phasic and tonic. Phasic LC activation is driven by the outcome of task-related decision processes and is p

aspirational.evidence_summary: The cited review provides a foundational theoretical basis for the claim by explicitly defining the phasic and tonic modes of LC-NE activity and their association with distinct behavioral states, directly supporting the definitional and correlational aspects of the roadmap.

citations.title: Nasal Respiration Entrains Human Limbic Oscillations and Modulates Cognitive Function
citations.abstract: The need to breathe links the mammalian olfactory system inextricably to the respiratory rhythms that draw air through the nose. In rodents and other small animals, slow oscillations of local field potential activity are driven at the rate of breathing (∼2-12 Hz) in olfactory bulb and cortex, and faster oscillatory bursts are coupled to specific phases of the respiratory cycle. These dynamic rhythms are thought to regulate cortical excitability and coordinate network interactions, helping to sha
citations.support_rationale: The abstract focuses on respiratory entrainment of limbic oscillations and cognitive function in humans with epilepsy, offering no evidence regarding the feasibility or difficulty of sample size calculations for the current study.

citations.support_rationale: This review synthesizes findings on respiratory-modulated brain oscillations and their link to psychiatric disorders, without mentioning statistical power, incidence rates, or sample size constraints.

citations.title: Increased Gamma Brainwave Amplitude Compared to Control in Three Different Meditation Traditions
citations.abstract: Despite decades of research, effects of different types of meditation on electroencephalographic (EEG) activity are still being defined. We compared practitioners of three different meditation traditions (Vipassana, Himalayan Yoga and Isha Shoonya) with a control group during a meditative and instructed mind-wandering (IMW) block. All meditators showed higher parieto-occipital 60-110 Hz gamma amplitude than control subjects as a trait effect observed during meditation and when considering medita
citations.support_rationale: This study directly compares Vipassana practitioners to controls and finds higher parieto-occipital gamma amplitude during meditation, supporting the claim about gamma rises in this specific tradition. While it does not explicitly measure 'perceptual clarity' in the abstract, it confirms the core physiological finding of increased gamma in the specified context.

citations.title: Occipital gamma activation during Vipassana meditation
citations.abstract: Long-term Vipassana meditators sat in meditation vs. a control rest (mind-wandering) state for 21 min in a counterbalanced design with spontaneous EEG recorded. Meditation state dynamics were measured with spectral decomposition of the last 6 min of the eyes-closed silent meditation compared to control state. Meditation was associated with a decrease in frontal delta (1-4 Hz) power, especially pronounced in those participants not reporting drowsiness during meditation. Relative increase in front
citations.support_rationale: This paper explicitly links increased parieto-occipital gamma power during eyes-closed Vipassana meditation to 'enhanced sensory awareness,' directly validating both the physiological mechanism and the proposed functional interpretation in the claim.
